ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for tomato braised beef brisket; closest parent 'beef dish' (FOODON_03302051) used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for tomato braised beef brisket; 'Beef, cooked' (A0CKP) is the closest defensible parent code.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 226529007 'Beef dish' used as closest parent; no specific code for tomato braised beef brisket exists.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of tomato braised beef brisket is approximately 300 g, comprising braised brisket pieces in tomato-based sauce, often served with steamed rice.

nutrient_source.title: Beef, brisket, braised, lean and fat — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 13326); supplemented with tomato-based sauce adjustment using USDA Foundation Foods tomato data
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Core macro and micronutrient values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy entry for braised beef brisket (NDB 13326), which represents the dominant ingredient by weight. Tomato sauce contribution (approximately 20% of dish weight) adds modest carbohydrate, sugar, vitamin C, and lycopene but does not materially alter the protein or fat profile per 100 g. Energy, protein, fat, and carbohydrate values are weighted estimates. Sodium is elevated to reflect soy sauce and seasoning typical of Chinese-style preparation, consistent with USDA Branded Food and FAO/INFOODS SEA reference ranges for braised Chinese beef dishes.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson FS et al., American Journal of Clinical Nutrition; beef and meat dishes GI reference; tomato-based stew analogue
gi_source.organization: American Journal of Clinical Nutrition / University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for tomato braised beef brisket. GI is estimated at 25 based on analogous meat-and-tomato stew entries in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2021 international GI tables. Meat dishes with low-carbohydrate content and tomato sauce consistently show GI in the 20–30 range. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (25 × 13.5) / 100 = 3.4, where carbohydrate per 300 g serving = 4.5 × 3 = 13.5 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from soy sauce and seasoning (~420 mg per 100 g; ~1260 mg per restaurant serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size and request reduced-soy-sauce preparation, as a single restaurant serving may contribute over 50% of the recommended daily sodium intake of 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (~25) and low carbohydrate content (~4.5 g per 100 g) with high protein supporting satiety
type2_diabetes.guidance: Tomato braised beef brisket has a low glycemic index and low glycemic load, making it a suitable protein-rich option for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed without high-GI accompaniments such as white rice in large portions.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate saturated fat (~3.6 g per 100 g) and elevated sodium; tomato provides lycopene with cardioprotective properties
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should moderate portion size due to saturated fat and sodium content, while noting that the tomato component contributes lycopene, a carotenoid associated with reduced cardiovascular risk in observational studies.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Tomato Braised Beef Brisket (番茄炖牛腩, Fānqié dùn niúnǎn) is a classic Chinese braised beef dish in which beef brisket is slow-cooked with fresh or canned tomatoes, soy sauce, ginger, garlic, star anise, and sugar until the meat is tender and the sauce is rich and slightly tangy-sweet. The dish is popular across mainland China, Hong Kong, and Singaporean Chinese restaurants, where it is commonly served as a main course alongside steamed white rice or noodles. The brisket, a cut from the lower chest of the cow, becomes exceptionally tender through prolonged braising, absorbing the umami-rich tomato sauce. The flavour profile is savoury, mildly sweet, and slightly acidic from the tomatoes, with aromatic depth from the spices. Nutritionally, the dish is a good source of high-quality protein (approximately 17.5 g per 100 g), iron (2.1 mg per 100 g), zinc (4.2 mg per 100 g), and vitamin B12 (1.8 mcg per 100 g), making it nutritionally dense. The fat content is moderate (~9.8 g per 100 g) with a meaningful saturated fat fraction (~3.6 g per 100 g). Sodium is elevated due to soy sauce seasoning (~420 mg per 100 g), which is a clinical consideration for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The glycemic index is low (~25), making it appropriate for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when portion-controlled. The tomato component contributes lycopene, vitamin C, and beta-carotene. This dish is not suitable for vegetarians, vegans, or those avoiding soy allergens. It is commonly found on the menus of Cantonese and Singaporean Chinese restaurants.
common_user_queries: How many calories in tomato braised beef brisket?
Is 番茄炖牛腩 good for diabetics?
Sodium content of Chinese beef brisket stew
Is tomato beef brisket high in fat?
Can I eat beef brisket with high blood pressure?
What is the GI of Chinese beef stew?
Protein content of braised beef brisket
Nutritional value of 番茄炖牛腩

confidence_reason: No exact entry for tomato braised beef brisket exists in HPB Singapore Foodo or ICMR-NIN. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy braised beef brisket data (NDB 13326) with a tomato sauce adjustment. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2021. Confidence is medium rather than low because the dominant ingredient (braised beef brisket) has strong USDA SR Legacy support and the adaptation methodology is transparent and defensible.
notes: Vitamin D (vitamin_d_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy source entry for braised beef brisket (NDB 13326) and is not reliably quantified in the tomato sauce component. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, trans fat, potassium, calcium, iron, cholesterol, vitamin C, vitamin A, B-vitamins, vitamin E, phosphorus, magnesium, zinc, selenium, and water values are drawn from USDA SR Legacy NDB 13326 with proportional adjustment for the tomato sauce fraction. Sodium is elevated above the USDA SR Legacy baseline to reflect soy sauce and seasoning typical of Chinese restaurant preparation, consistent with FAO/INFOODS SEA reference ranges. The dish is listed on field survey data at Wan Jia Fu Chinese Restaurant (万家福创新中国菜), Circular Road, at SGD 27.80. The food_name in the input ('Tomato Stir-Fried Beef Brisket') has been corrected to 'Tomato Braised Beef Brisket' as the Chinese name 番茄炖牛腩 specifically denotes braising/stewing (炖), not stir-frying (炒). The input alias is preserved in aliases_by_language.english and the flat aliases list. Halal status is marked false as the restaurant's halal certification status is not confirmed from the input data.
